notes: Build-in-public: technical story of integrating with Omani banks for WPS. The undocumented edge cases nobody warns you about.
title: Building WPS payroll integration with Oman's banking system — the undocumented parts
brief: Build-in-public: technical story of integrating with Omani banks for WPS. The undocumented edge cases nobody warns you about.
body:
Nobody told us the WPS file format changes by bank.

Same Ministry of Manpower specification. Same WPS standard. But BankMuscat wants the employee record fields in a different order than National Bank of Oman. And both reject files with a trailing newline in a specific column without telling you which column.

We found this out the hard way — through a customer's payroll rejection at 11:47pm on the last working day of the month.

Here's what building a real WPS integration actually involves, if you're doing it for Oman:

**The spec is a starting point, not a spec.**
The Ministry of Manpower publishes the WPS technical standard. It is correct. It is also incomplete. Each bank has its own quirks — field lengths, encoding expectations, timestamp formats — that you discover through trial and rejection.

**Validation must happen before submission.**
A rejected file doesn't just fail. It triggers a Ministry of Manpower non-compliance flag against the employer. The flag can affect work permit renewals. We rebuilt our entire validation pipeline after our first production rejection to catch bank-specific issues before the file leaves our system.

**IBAN validation is a separate problem.**
Oman IBANs are 23 characters. We found three different formats in customer data — with space separators, without, and one mystery format from a legacy system that turned out to be a Saudi IBAN from a previous entity. WPS silently fails on wrong IBANs in some banks. It errors loudly in others.

**The end-of-month clustering problem.**
When you're running payroll for 200 companies, every one of them submits in the same 48-hour window. API rate limits are not documented. You find them when your scheduler starts dropping requests.

We now run per-tenant isolated payroll jobs with explicit timezone context, staged over a 6-hour window, with validation, retry logic, and bank-specific format adapters.

None of this is clever. All of it was expensive to learn.

If you're building anything that touches WPS in Oman — DM me. Happy to share what we know.

#BuildInPublic #SaaS #GCCTech #TypeScript #SmartPROHub
